label: SES-052 close_out_payload entity schema designed; reconciled push convention across surfaces: 1 session, 6 decisions (DEC-147..152), 0 planning items, 7 references

session.title: close_out_payload entity schema designed (fifth-of-six per-entity schema-design conversation); push convention reconciled across Claude.ai project instructions, userMemories, and repo-level CLAUDE.md files
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai schema-design conversation that produced governance-schema-specs/close_out_payload.md (440 lines at v1.0) plus working-conventions sections in both crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md and ClevelandBusinessMentoring/CLAUDE.md documenting the push convention bifurcation between Claude.ai sandbox and Claude Code surfaces. Doug's opening directive was to process the kickoff prompt autonomously using default decisions and not ask for input unless critical; the entire spec was drafted and committed under that directive. After the spec landed, Doug surfaced a missing-commit problem (the local commit had not been pushed), which traced to a contradiction between the project-level Claude.ai instruction 'You commit, I push' and a userMemories entry encoding a context-dependent split. Resolution: project-level instruction updated by Doug to describe Claude.ai sandbox behavior only; userMemories entry 16 tightened; new Working conventions section added to both repo-level CLAUDE.md files so Claude Code (which has no visibility into Claude.ai project instructions or userMemories) can see the rule. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the close_out_payload entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/close_out_payload.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md."

The conversation was directed to process autonomously per Doug's opening turn, so the structured-architectural-discussion cadence collapsed into a single drafting pass with one principal-decision pause point.

Pre-flight context gathering. Sparse-cloned crmbuilder; read governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, workstream.md, conversation.md, reference_book.md, work_ticket.md in full; read DEC-117 and DEC-118 from db-export/decisions.json; inventoried close-out-payloads/ directory (ses_001.json through ses_051.json, all sharing the four-section payload shape: label, session, decisions, planning_items, references); read CLAUDE.md head; verified next-available identifiers (SES-052, DEC-147..152, no new PI — PI-022 covers backfill, PI-023 already applied).

Principal decision surfaced and locked: payload content representation. Four options examined — JSON column for in-DB queryability, text column with on-demand parse, file-path with metadata fields, hybrid file-plus-snapshot. Lock: file-path only, no content column. Justified by the cross-spec precedent locked by DEC-139 (reference_book) and DEC-145 (work_ticket); the payload is structurally a file-tracking entity identical to work_ticket; the JSON content lives authoritatively in git; the apply script reads the file directly; future content-query needs are satisfied by a derived-view endpoint that parses file_path on demand. The two-part test was passed but the cross-spec precedent narrowed the options decisively enough that the question did not warrant a separate interrupt.

Remaining decisions locked without interrupt under the autonomous-processing directive: identifier prefix COP (three letters, matches DEC/SES/REF precedent; two-letter alternatives ambiguous); lifecycle five-status workflow drafted→ready→applied with terminals cancelled and superseded, parallel to work_ticket; applied-requires-edge rule realizing the inverse-pattern precedent locked by DEC-143; production-edge required at all statuses (the family-3 definition that each payload is produced by exactly one conversation); one new relationship-kind close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation; declines close_out_payload_kind enum (single-kind entity) and close_out_payload_schema_version column (structure stable across 50+ historical files); standard API surface with no deviations; default UI plus Status filter combo (parallel to work_ticket's pattern); soft-delete with restore explicitly distinct from cancelled lifecycle status and applied terminal status.

Validation gate per spec guide §7.1 passed: all nine subsections of §3 present, 16 acceptance criteria, 12 categorized open questions (5 build, 2 backfill, 5 future), all required cross-references present (workstream plan, spec guide, foundation decisions DEC-117..122, prior schemas), zero TBDs. Committed and pushed at 87c7c16.

Post-spec reconciliation. Doug flagged the missing commit on GitHub — sandbox commit had not been pushed. Investigation surfaced the contradiction between project-level instruction 'You commit, I push' (unconditional) and userMemories entry 16 (context-dependent split: sandbox=Claude pushes, Claude Code=Doug pushes). Reconciled in three steps: (1) Doug clarified the rule operationally — Claude.ai sandbox is inaccessible to Doug so commits without pushes are lost; Claude Code commits to Doug's local clone where Doug reviews before pushing. (2) userMemories entry 16 was replaced with the tightened wording. (3) Doug updated the Claude.ai project instructions on his side. (4) Claude observed that Claude Code has no visibility into Claude.ai project instructions or userMemories — both halves of the rule must live in repo-level CLAUDE.md to reach Claude Code. New 'Working conventions' section added to both crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md (commit 536df25) and ClevelandBusinessMentoring/CLAUDE.md (commit 00c0bda) with identical text documenting the bifurcation.

Close-out artifact authoring. This payload (ses_052.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-052.md) authored at conversation close. The session record was authored as part of the payload bundle per the operative SES-046-through-SES-051 precedent (the kickoff's 'desktop dialog' language was descriptive of one available mechanism but not prescriptive; the bundle-in-payload mechanism is the proven path).
session.artifacts_produced: Four files committed and pushed in this conversation: (1) PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/close_out_payload.md (new — 440-line v1.0 schema specification, the fifth-of-six per-entity governance schemas, committed at 87c7c16); (2) CLAUDE.md (modified in crmbuilder repo — new Working conventions section under H2 with push-convention bifurcation between Claude.ai sandbox and Claude Code surfaces, committed at 536df25); (3) ClevelandBusinessMentoring/CLAUDE.md (modified in CBM repo — parallel Working conventions addition with identical text, committed at 00c0bda); plus this close-out payload (close-out-payloads/ses_052.json) and the apply prompt (prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-052.md) committed together as the close-out artifacts. Six decision records (DEC-147..152) and seven references land via this payload's apply prompt. No source code modified; this is a pure planning conversation with a documentation-edits side-track. No other PRDs touched. The CBM repo edit is the only commit outside crmbuilder for this conversation.
session.in_flight_at_end: The deposit_event schema-design conversation opens next against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-deposit-event.md — the sixth and final per-entity schema-design conversation in the workstream. It inherits the seven cross-spec precedents now in force (the six from SES-048 through SES-051 plus the applied-requires-edge realization locked by this conversation as DEC-149). The deposit_event entity is structurally close_out_payload's family-3 counterpart — the apply-event record that records each close_out_payload apply. Primary architectural question for that conversation: whether deposit_event adopts pure append-only (no PUT, no PATCH, no DELETE) or admits soft-delete for record-management cleanup; this conversation deliberately did not decide that question. After the sixth schema-design conversation closes, the workstream's build-planning conversation consumes the six schema specifications and produces the integrating Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, and per-slice execution prompts. Multi-tenancy routing fix slice B remains pending in parallel as a separate workstream. Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 still waits on its own sub-domain hierarchy amendment (Planning Item 001 in the CBM engagement) as a separate planning conversation. PI-022 (retroactive backfill) continues to cover this conversation's records implicitly, as it does for all governance entity workstream records.

decisions.title: close_out_payload entity identifier prefix and format: COP-NNN, three-letter form, no collision with existing prefixes
decisions.context: Per the spec guide section 6, identifier prefixes must be 2 to 5 uppercase letters and must not collide with existing prefixes. The kickoff prompt named COP as the working assumption; alternatives examined were two-letter forms (CO, CP) and four-to-five-letter forms (CLOUT, PYLD, CLOSE). The existing prefix set: DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC, ENG, WS, CONV, RB, WT. DEC-123 affirmed each downstream conversation makes its own prefix-length call within the 2-to-5-letter range without locking the cross-spec norm at any particular length.
decisions.decision: **Adopt COP as the close_out_payload entity identifier prefix.** Three letters, matching the most common existing prefix length (DEC, SES, RSK, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT). Identifier format: COP-NNN, zero-padded to three digits (COP-001, COP-046, etc.). The two-letter form CO was rejected for ambiguity (reads as Company or Carbon monoxide); CP was rejected as too generic. Four- and five-letter alternatives (CLOUT, PYLD, CLOSE) add length without disambiguation gain. COP survives the collision check against the full existing prefix list.
decisions.rationale: Three-letter is the dominant precedent length and reads cleanly with the standard NNN suffix. Per DEC-123's affirmation that each downstream conversation chooses its own prefix length, three letters here is appropriate without binding the deposit_event conversation to any particular choice on its own facts.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — two-letter CO or CP.** Rejected for ambiguity (CO reads as Company; CP is too generic). **Option (c) — four-letter PYLD, CLOSE, CLOUT.** Rejected as length-without-disambiguation; the three-letter form is unambiguous against the existing prefix set. **Option (d) — five-letter CLOSEOUT.** Rejected as visually cluttering when paired with the NNN suffix.
decisions.consequences: All close_out_payload records use the COP-NNN identifier pattern. The access layer's identifier-validation regex is ^COP-\d{3}$. The next-identifier helper endpoint returns {"next": "COP-NNN"} for the next available number. The cross-spec consistency check before build-planning verifies no collision against any other governance-entity prefix introduced by the six schemas (workstream uses WS, conversation uses CONV, reference_book uses RB, work_ticket uses WT, close_out_payload uses COP, deposit_event chooses its own at its conversation).

decisions.title: close_out_payload content representation: repo-relative file_path pointer only, no payload_content column
decisions.context: The kickoff prompt named this as the principal design question — how the structured JSON payload is represented in the database. Four options surfaced: (a) JSON column storing the payload directly for in-database queryability; (b) text column with on-demand parse; (c) file-path-with-metadata fields where the file remains the source of truth; (d) hybrid file-plus-snapshot. The choice has consequences for how the build-planning conversation's apply path interacts with the payload entity. All 50+ historical payloads under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ live as JSON files; the apply script (apply_close_out.py) reads the file directly.
decisions.decision: **Adopt option (c): repo-relative close_out_payload_file_path column as the only payload-content representation; no payload_content JSON column on the entity table.** The payload's authoritative content lives in the file under git, exactly as reference_book records' content does. The database carries the governance view (identifier, title, description, status, lifecycle timestamps, references to producing conversation and applying deposit_event). The apply script continues to read the file at apply time; future content-query needs are satisfied by a derived-view endpoint (provisional path GET /close-out-payloads/{identifier}/content) that reads file_path on demand and parses the JSON, not by a stored column. The schema admits the future endpoint additively without requiring a table change.
decisions.rationale: The cross-spec precedent is decisive. DEC-139 (reference_book) and DEC-145 (work_ticket) both adopted repo-relative file_path with no content column — file-tracking governance entities store the path, not the content. Close_out_payload is structurally identical in shape: a file at a deterministic location under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/, produced once, applied once, audited via git history. The fact that the content is JSON rather than markdown is a content-format detail, not a governance-shape difference. Denormalizing a 25-to-60 KB JSON blob into a database column per record adds storage cost, mutation-vs-file-integrity questions, and write-time validation complexity for no first-class query need that the file itself does not already satisfy.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (a) — JSON column.** Rejected as denormalization of content that lives authoritatively on the filesystem. Adds a write-time consistency burden (database column may drift from file) and a column-vs-file integrity question on every read. **Option (b) — text column with parse.** Rejected as worst-of-both-worlds: same storage cost as (a) without the in-database query benefit. **Option (d) — hybrid file plus snapshot column.** Rejected as redundancy under git's existing audit guarantees; the snapshot adds maintenance burden (when does the snapshot get refreshed? how does it stay in sync with the file?) for marginal benefit. The derived-view endpoint pattern named in the chosen option supplies content-query convenience without the synchronization burden.
decisions.consequences: The close_out_payloads table carries one file-pointer column (close_out_payload_file_path) with the same repo-relative validation rules as work_ticket_file_path and reference_book_file_path. The same physical file may legally coexist as a file_path value across all three tables (work_ticket, reference_book, close_out_payload) under the section-2 boundary discipline; uniqueness is within each table only. The apply script's evolution to consume payload content from the file (current behaviour) versus from a database lookup of file_path (alternative behaviour) is a build-planning concern — both are consistent with this schema. The future derived-view endpoint, if shipped, is non-breaking; no table change.

decisions.title: close_out_payload workflow-shaped lifecycle: five statuses with truly terminal terminals, applied-requires-edge rule realizing the SES-051 precedent, supersession-requires-edge rule, production-edge required at all statuses
decisions.context: The kickoff prompt named the lifecycle questions as four sub-questions: status set, append-only-vs-soft-delete, schema version field, and inbound-edge requirements for terminal states. The cross-spec precedents in force at conversation open: DEC-125 (truly-terminal terminals, supersession-requires-outbound-edge), DEC-131 (multi-state workflow-shaped lifecycle for governance entities that need it), DEC-143 (terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge — work_ticket's consumed-requires-edge, explicitly named close_out_payload's applied-terminal as the precedent's first downstream application).
decisions.decision: **Adopt a five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle: drafted → ready → applied, plus terminals cancelled and superseded.** Default starter status is drafted. The applied terminal requires an inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload edge (realizing DEC-143's inverse-pattern precedent on this entity's facts). The superseded terminal requires an outgoing supersedes edge to another close_out_payload (mirroring DEC-125's pattern verbatim). The production-linkage edge close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation is required at all statuses regardless of lifecycle state — including drafted — because the family-3 definition (per DEC-117) is that a payload is produced at a conversation's close; the producing conversation is part of the payload's identity. All three terminal states (applied, cancelled, superseded) are truly terminal — no transitions out, including transitions between terminal states. Re-application of a payload is modelled as a new payload that supersedes the prior; reactivation as a status transition is not supported.
decisions.rationale: The shape parallels work_ticket exactly because the governance roles are structural mirrors: work_ticket has drafted → ready → consumed; close_out_payload has drafted → ready → applied. Both have terminals cancelled and superseded. Both have a single non-starter terminal (the successful-use outcome) requiring a specific external-act edge to be present. The truly-terminal posture inherited from workstream and conversation prevents audit-blurring (an applied payload becoming un-applied would break the deposit_event's referent). The production-edge-required-at-all-statuses rule realizes the family-3 definition cleanly: a payload without a producing conversation is malformed by DEC-117's definition, regardless of how complete the payload file itself is.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — pure append-only with no soft-delete.** Rejected because legitimate record-cleanup operations exist (e.g., removing a stale draft authored by mistake). Append-only fits the deposit_event side of the family-3 pair — the apply-outcome record — not the payload side which is mutable in pre-apply states. **Option (c) — three-status simplification (drafted → applied; cancelled and superseded collapsed into a single 'abandoned' terminal).** Rejected because cancelled and superseded carry different governance semantics — cancelled has no successor; superseded names one. Collapsing them loses the distinction between 'discarded' and 'replaced by'. **Option (d) — admit pause/resume by allowing transitions out of cancelled.** Rejected per the truly-terminal precedent established by workstream and conversation. A payload that needs to resume is a new payload that supersedes the prior; the audit trail prefers two records with a supersession edge over one record with a non-monotonic history.
decisions.consequences: The close_out_payloads table carries four per-status lifecycle timestamp columns (ready_at, applied_at, cancelled_at, superseded_at) in addition to the three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at). The access layer enforces: status-transition validation per the lifecycle map; applied-requires-inbound-edge (HTTP 422 if attempted without the deposit_event edge); supersession-requires-outbound-edge (HTTP 422 if attempted without the supersedes edge); production-edge-required-at-all-statuses (HTTP 422 if attempted without exactly one outbound close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation edge); single-producer rule (HTTP 422 if more than one production edge attempts to coexist). Soft-delete is orthogonal to lifecycle state; a record can be in any of the five lifecycle statuses and additionally soft-deleted. This conversation does NOT introduce a new cross-spec precedent — every rule follows an inherited precedent applied to this entity's facts. The append-only-vs-soft-delete posture for deposit_event is deferred to that conversation.

decisions.title: close_out_payload production-linkage via references-edge with new kind close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation; declines typed sequencing kind in favour of generic supersedes
decisions.context: Every close_out_payload is produced at exactly one conversation's close (per DEC-117's family-3 definition). The kickoff prompt named this relationship's mechanism as an open question: foreign-key column on the close_out_payloads table versus references-table edge. The cross-spec precedent locked by DEC-124 and DEC-130 is references-edge over foreign-key for all parent-child governance relationships. Separately, the question of whether close_out_payload sequencing (one payload replaced by another for the same conversation) should be typed (e.g., close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload) or use the generic supersedes kind was governed by DEC-133's frequency-justified test.
decisions.decision: **Production-linkage lives in the references table with a new relationship kind close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation; cardinality is exactly-one outbound edge per close_out_payload record, enforced at the access layer at every status including drafted.** A conversation may be the target of zero or more such edges (zero if not yet closed; one in the typical single-payload-per-conversation case; more than one if drafts were superseded and replaced). The new relationship-kind vocabulary entry is registered in REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS, with the _kinds_for_pair clause binding it to the (close_out_payload, conversation) pair only. The ENTITY_TYPES set adds close_out_payload (conversation is already in ENTITY_TYPES from SES-049's registration). Same-type supersession reuses the existing generic supersedes kind for the (close_out_payload, close_out_payload) pair — no new typed kind close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload is introduced.
decisions.rationale: References-edge for parent-child governance relationships is the locked cross-spec precedent. No reason on this entity's facts to deviate. The production relationship is naturally one-edge-one-kind, consistent with conversation's belongs_to_workstream pattern and work_ticket's inbound-from-conversation pattern. Per DEC-133's frequency-justified test, typed sequencing kinds are introduced when the entity-family's sequencing is structurally frequent. Close_out_payload sequencing (one draft replaced by another) is rare in practice — most conversations produce exactly one payload that is then applied. The frequency-justified test points at generic supersedes for the same-type pair, consistent with workstream's posture and unlike conversation's (where the every-conversation-belongs-to-a-predecessor-or-successor frequency justified the typed kind).
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — foreign-key column conversation_id on the close_out_payloads table.** Rejected per the references-edge-over-FK cross-spec precedent. Modifying the conversations table or adding columnar coupling between governance entities is the pattern the precedent specifically declines. **Option (c) — typed close_out_payload_succeeds_close_out_payload sequencing kind.** Rejected per DEC-133's frequency-justified test; sequencing in the close_out_payload family is rare enough that the generic supersedes kind suffices and the typed kind would add vocabulary noise without query value. If a real frequency emerges, the typed kind is a one-line vocab.py addition in a future workstream.
decisions.consequences: One new relationship-kind vocabulary entry close_out_payload_produced_by_conversation is added by the build-planning conversation alongside the other governance schemas' additions, with the matching _kinds_for_pair clause and the Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind's CHECK constraint. The access layer enforces exactly-one-outbound-edge cardinality at all statuses for this kind. The inbound deposit_event_applies_close_out_payload kind from the next conversation's entity is listed informationally in this spec's section 3.3.2 only; its vocab.py registration and access-layer at-most-one-inbound cardinality enforcement belong to deposit_event.md. Same-type supersession is admitted automatically once close_out_payload is in ENTITY_TYPES because the existing _kinds_for_pair rule for same-type pairs admits supersedes.

decisions.title: close_out_payload field inventory including four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines close_out_payload_kind enum (single-kind entity) and close_out_payload_schema_version column (structure stable)
decisions.context: The kickoff prompt named the field-inventory question and three specific sub-questions: whether to include a close_out_payload_kind classification enum (since other state-write package variants might emerge), whether to include a close_out_payload_schema_version field tracking payload structural version, and whether to denormalize record-counts-by-kind metadata into columnar fields. The existing 50+ payload files all share the four-section shape (label, session, decisions, planning_items, references) with no version field on the payload content itself.
decisions.decision: **Adopt the eleven-column shape: close_out_payload_identifier, close_out_payload_title, close_out_payload_description, close_out_payload_notes, close_out_payload_status, close_out_payload_file_path, plus three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at) and four per-status lifecycle timestamps (ready_at, applied_at, cancelled_at, superseded_at).** Decline a close_out_payload_kind enum because every record in this table is the same kind (the single artifact type close-out-payload-at-conversation-close); the kind enum has no taxonomic axis to express. Decline a close_out_payload_schema_version column because the payload's JSON structure has been stable across all 50+ historical files; tracking schema version is premature optimization. Decline denormalized record-counts-by-kind columns; the file_path read at use time supplies the counts when needed.
decisions.rationale: The eleven-column shape parallels work_ticket's field inventory verbatim (work_ticket has eleven columns too: identifier, title, description, notes, kind, status, file_path, three base timestamps, four per-status lifecycle timestamps). Two structural differences worth noting: work_ticket has a kind enum (four values: kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other) while close_out_payload has no kind enum because no taxonomic axis exists; close_out_payload uses a different status set (drafted/ready/applied vs. work_ticket's drafted/ready/consumed) reflecting the different terminal-state external act (apply vs. consume). The decline of payload_schema_version is principled: the schema admits the column as a one-line additive migration if structure ever evolves, and pre-adding the column on the speculation that structure will evolve violates minimum-viable scope. The decline of record-counts-by-kind columns reflects the same scoping discipline as the file_path-only content-representation decision (DEC-148): the file under git carries the authoritative content; derived views compute counts from it on demand.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — add close_out_payload_kind enum with values like 'session_close_out' and 'bulk_import_payload'.** Rejected because no other variants exist or are currently anticipated. Adding the enum now would require populating every record with the same value indefinitely; adding it later (when a second variant actually emerges) is a one-line additive migration. **Option (c) — add close_out_payload_schema_version column with default 1.0 for all historical records.** Rejected as premature; the JSON structure has been stable across 50+ files. **Option (d) — denormalize record-counts-by-kind (decisions_count, planning_items_count, references_count) into columnar fields.** Rejected because the values are easily computed from the file on demand and would require a write-time consistency burden between file content and column values.
decisions.consequences: The close_out_payloads table has exactly eleven columns. No kind enum. No schema_version column. No counts columns. Future additive migrations remain available for any of these if real-use signal supports them; the schema accommodates them without breaking existing records. The detail pane in the desktop UI does not render a Kind row (since the field does not exist); the master pane's column list does not include a Kind column for the same reason. If a derived-view endpoint exposing payload content is shipped in a future build, it can compute and return record-counts-by-kind from the file at request time.

decisions.title: close_out_payload API surface, default UI with Status filter combo addition, soft-delete posture distinct from cancelled lifecycle status, sixteen acceptance criteria
decisions.context: The remaining design questions after lifecycle, relationships, fields, and content representation were settled: which API endpoints to expose, which UI deviations from default the entity needs, what soft-delete semantics apply, and what acceptance criteria define correct implementation. The cross-spec precedents for these questions were locked by DEC-128 (workstream — standard defaults), DEC-134 (conversation — standard defaults with one natural addition), DEC-140 (reference_book — standard API with three version-management sub-endpoints, Kind column and version-history detail pane addition), and DEC-146 (work_ticket — standard API with Kind column and Status filter combo additions).
decisions.decision: **Adopt the standard V2 endpoint set with no deviations: GET /close-out-payloads (with ?include_deleted and ?status query filters), GET /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, POST /close-out-payloads, PUT /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, PATCH /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, DELETE /close-out-payloads/{identifier}, POST /close-out-payloads/{identifier}/restore, GET /close-out-payloads/next-identifier.** All endpoints use the {data, meta, errors} envelope. Identifier auto-assignment server-side on POST omission. The desktop UI uses the default layout with one natural addition: a Status filter combo in the master-pane toolbar (parallel to work_ticket's addition), because browse-by-status is the operational pattern for payloads — ready to see what is queued for apply, applied to see what has been deposited. No Kind column in the master pane (no kind field exists). Soft-delete is the default behaviour and is explicitly orthogonal to lifecycle state: cancelled is a lifecycle outcome (the payload was abandoned without being applied; the record remains visible in list endpoints), applied is a successful-use outcome (the deposit_event records the apply; the record remains visible), soft-deleted is a record-management operation (the consultant removed the record from default views). Sixteen acceptance criteria captured covering schema migration, identifier validation, status-transition validation, the three terminal-states-truly-terminal cases, the three edge-requirement rules (applied/superseded/production), per-status timestamp server-set behaviour, file-path validation, access-layer method signatures, REST endpoint shape, identifier-auto-assignment race-freedom, soft-delete round-trip, vocabulary registration, sidebar position, master pane columns, detail pane rendering, and end-to-end backfill of a representative historical payload.
decisions.rationale: Standard defaults plus one natural addition is the established posture for governance entities with operational browse patterns (work_ticket and reference_book both did this on their own facts). The Status filter combo addition is naturally justified by the close_out_payload's operational pattern: a consultant scanning the entity catalog wants to see 'what is queued for apply' (status=ready) or 'what has been applied' (status=applied) at a glance. Soft-delete as the posture for the payload side of the family-3 pair (with append-only naturally fitting deposit_event on the other side, deferred to that conversation) is consistent with DEC-013's framing of when append-only is the right pattern — append-only fits transactional records (a decision, a session, a deposit event), not state-write packages with legitimate pre-apply mutability.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — pure append-only after POST, no PUT/PATCH/DELETE.** Rejected because legitimate pre-apply mutation cases exist (correcting a typo in description before the payload is finalized as ready; updating notes after the payload is authored but before it is applied). **Option (c) — collapse Status filter combo into the inherited 'show deleted' toggle.** Rejected because the two filters do different work (lifecycle status vs. soft-delete state); combining them would conflate orthogonal concepts. **Option (d) — defer the Status filter combo to a future UI release.** Rejected because the operational pattern is structurally part of the entity's day-one use (the very first reason to browse the close_out_payloads catalog is to see what is queued for apply), and adding the combo at build-time is a small ListDetailPanel filter machinery extension matching work_ticket's pattern.
decisions.consequences: The build-planning conversation produces a build slice for close_out_payload that mirrors work_ticket's build slice in shape: one Alembic migration creating the close_out_payloads table; one access-layer module with the eight standard methods; one FastAPI router with the eight endpoints plus the next-identifier helper; one desktop panel using ListDetailPanel with the four columns and the Status filter combo toolbar addition; one detail pane rendering the eleven fields plus the ReferencesSection widget with the family-defining production-edge prominently displayed; create/edit/delete/restore dialogs matching the v0.3 pattern; sixteen test cases mapped to the acceptance criteria. Vocabulary additions consolidated with the other governance schemas' additions into one vocab.py update plus one Alembic migration on refs.relationship_kind's CHECK constraint. No deviations from V2's standard API or UI architecture; the close_out_payload panel reads, looks, and behaves like the other governance-entity panels.
